rule_text: A trader should sell out-of-the-money calls when they believe the underlying will reach a moderate upside target but remain below the call's strike price, capturing full premium decay if the call expires worthless.

Trader's upside target = T
Call strike price = K
T < K - 2.5% of S
Current call premium = P > 0.25
exit_conditions: Underlying reaches upside target T
50% of premium collected (take profit early)
Underlying approaches strike K with 10+ days to expiration

rule_text: A trader should match long option strike selection to directional conviction and risk tolerance, as the strike price determines both the maximum loss and break-even point of the position.
entry_conditions: Bullish directional bias identified
Select call strike at or 1-2% in-the-money relative to spot price
Maximum loss tolerance = premium paid ≤ 1% of account
Probability of profit target ≥ 75%
exit_conditions: Exit at 50% of maximum profit
Exit if spot closes below strike + 50% of premium paid
Exit at expiration

rule_text: A trader should match their position structure (buying vs. selling options) to their risk tolerance, because option buyers have limited risk with unlimited reward potential, while option sellers have limited reward with unlimited risk potential.
entry_conditions: Position type = long option only
Maximum loss per trade = premium paid
Account risk per trade ≤ 1% of portfolio
exit_conditions: Exit when loss reaches 100% of premium (total loss)
Exit at 50% max profit target
Exit at expiration or 7 days before expiration

rule_text: A trader should match their position structure (buying vs. selling options) to their risk tolerance, because option buyers have limited risk with unlimited reward potential, while option sellers have limited reward with unlimited risk potential.
entry_conditions: Position type = short option or short spread
Minimum capital requirement = 20x the maximum defined loss
Account risk per trade ≤ 5% of portfolio
exit_conditions: Exit at 50% of max profit target immediately
Use stop loss at 200% of premium collected
Exit 7-10 days before expiration to avoid assignment

rule_text: A trader should establish a straddle position when the expected price movement range at expiration exceeds the total premium paid, ensuring breakeven occurs only at extreme price levels with unlimited profit potential beyond those levels.
entry_conditions: Long call and long put at ATM strike established simultaneously
Total premium paid ≤ 40% of strike price
Expected move at expiration > 1.5x total premium paid
exit_conditions: Exit if either leg reaches 6.40 (or 1x premium equivalent)
Exit at expiration or when one leg exceeds premium paid

rule_text: A trader should construct a call spread (long lower strike, short higher strike) when bullish on the underlying, accepting reduced maximum profit in exchange for lower capital requirement and improved breakeven compared to outright call purchase.
entry_conditions: Bullish trend signal confirmed
Breakeven point set at 50% of distance between current price and short call strike
Spread width = 5 points or less
exit_conditions: Exit at 50% max profit
Exit if underlying falls below long call strike
Exit at 21 DTE if max profit not reached

rule_text: A trader buying options must be correct on both market direction AND speed of price movement; buying options requires predictive accuracy on two dimensions simultaneously, making it riskier than directional futures trading.
entry_conditions: Implied volatility percentile < 25th percentile (low volatility regime)
Expected move magnitude > 2× at-the-money straddle price
Time to expiration ≥ 30 days (allows for slower moves)
Directional conviction score ≥ 8/10
exit_conditions: Position reaches +50% profit target
Time decay reduces value by >40% without directional movement
Directional thesis breaks (reverse entry signal)

rule_text: A trader should only initiate a long underlying position when the expected return exceeds the carrying cost over the intended holding period, otherwise the position cannot achieve profitability.
entry_conditions: Expected return calculated from price distribution > carrying cost × 1.50
Holding period defined in advance
Carrying cost explicitly calculated (financing rate + storage/dividend costs)
exit_conditions: Position exit if expected return falls below 1.25× carrying cost
Stop at holding period end regardless of P&L

rule_text: A trader should use volatility-based expected move calculations (dividing by 16 for daily or 7.2 for weekly) to estimate the probability distribution of underlying price moves over short time horizons, despite carrying costs, because the effect of carry and compounding is negligible over short periods.
entry_conditions: Implied volatility calculated via standard method
Expected move computed using divisor method (÷16 daily or ÷7.2 weekly)
Underlying price move observed within 66% confidence band (1 std dev, ~2 weeks out of 3)
exit_conditions: Price moves beyond 95% confidence band (2 std dev, ~19 weeks out of 20)
Time decay reaches 50% of position theta

90 Chapter 5
How likely is an immediate reevaluation in implied volatility from 14.6% to 18.3%?
While violent changes in implied volatility are possible, they are the exception rather
than the rule. C
rule_text: A trader should anticipate gradual implied volatility adjustments aligned with observed changes in underlying price volatility, rather than expecting immediate discrete jumps.
entry_conditions: Realized volatility of underlying > implied volatility by at least 3.0%
Deviation persists for at least 5 consecutive trading days
No recent earnings or major events within 2 weeks
exit_conditions: Implied volatility has converged within 1.5% of realized volatility
Realized volatility drops below implied volatility
Position duration exceeds 30 days

rule_text: Use gamma as a predictive tool to anticipate delta drift from expected price moves, then adjust hedge sizing proactively before market opening rather than relying on static delta at entry.

Gamma position is non-zero (long or short)
Expected price move magnitude ≥ 1.5 × (20-day ATR or IV-implied range)
Trader has explicit directional forecast for open
exit_conditions: Market opens and actual move materializes
Rehedge to new delta target derived from (original_delta + gamma × actual_move)

Time to expiration >= 45 days
exit_conditions: Exit 50% at +200% profit
Exit remaining at +400% profit or stop at -50% loss

rule_text: Exploit time decay differential in calendar spreads by buying shorter-term options and selling longer-term options of the same type and strike, since near-term options decay faster than far-term options when the underlying price remains stable.
entry_conditions: Underlying price stability: no movement > 0.5% in prior month
Time difference between legs: 1-2 months (e.g., March vs May)
Both options at-the-money or near-the-money
Implied volatility rank below 40th percentile
exit_conditions: Exit when spread reaches 50% of max profit
Exit if underlying moves > 1% against direction
Exit 2 weeks before near-term expiration

Realized volatility expected to remain suppressed relative to IV

Close negative gamma if IV percentile rises above 40th
Exit if theta decay becomes negative (time working against position)

VERTICAL SPREADS
It is common for traders to take bull or bear market positions by choosing appropriate
ratio spreads, butterflies, or time spreads, but in each of these positions volat
rule_text: A trader prioritizing directional conviction should select spreads where delta stability (directional characteristics preservation) is the primary concern and volatility sensitivity is secondary, ensuring the spread maintains its intended directional bias across market moves.
entry_conditions: Directional view is high conviction (trader has strong market outlook)
Vega exposure of candidate spread is minimal (vega < 10% of delta notional)
Delta remains positive/negative across IV range of ±10 vol points
Gamma exposure is near-zero or negative (reducing unintended convexity)
exit_conditions: Delta flips sign or deviates >5% from entry delta
Implied volatility moves >10 points beyond stress threshold
Time decay erodes >20% of initial edge

rule_text: A trader should adjust spread delta expectations and hedge positioning based on volatility forecast accuracy, because spread deltas converge toward 50 when realized volatility exceeds estimates and diverge away from 50 when realized volatility falls short of estimates.
entry_conditions: Implied volatility estimate established at position entry
Realized volatility calculated over rolling 5-day window
Absolute difference between realized and implied volatility ≥ 3%

parameters.delta_shift_assumption: deltas move away from 50 (low vol) or toward 50 (high vol)

exit_conditions: Rehedge spread by adjusting long/short ratio to restore target delta
Close position if forecast error persists beyond 10 trading days

rule_text: A trader should construct a zero-cost or positive-expected-value spread by identifying and exploiting relative mispricing between two related spreads, purchasing the underpriced spread and selling the overpriced spread regardless of directional bias, when both spreads have identical market prices but differ in intrinsic theoretical value.

Implied volatility term structure is monotonic or flat (no inversion)
exit_conditions: Position achieves target profit of 0.25 cents or more
Bid-ask spread widens beyond 0.20 cents
Liquidity dries up and cannot unwind within 1 minute

rule_text: A trader holding an American option should actively reposition (exercise, sell and buy stock substitute, or exit entirely) before a dividend ex-date rather than passively hold, because holding exposes the position to certain loss equal to the dividend amount due to parity collapse in the option price.

Option delta >= 0.95 (near stock parity equivalence)
Known dividend ex-date within 5 trading days
Option premium within 1% of intrinsic value (at/near parity)
exit_conditions: Execute repositioning action (exercise, sell-buy stock, or exit position entirely) before market close ex-dividend day

rule_text: A trader should execute a short stock + short deep ITM put spread to capture risk-free interest on the combined exercise/sale proceeds, holding until put assignment or expiration.
entry_conditions: Short stock at market price S
Simultaneously sell puts with strike K where K >= 0.98*S (98%+ of stock price)
Put expiration <= 30 days
Interest rate differential > 2% annualized
Transaction costs (round-trip) < 0.5% of notional
exit_conditions: Exit immediately upon put assignment (break-even by design)
Exit at expiration if puts unexercised (capture full interest accrual)
Exit if puts move ITM more than 1% to lock profit early

rule_text: A trader must execute closing trades at precise exchange closing prices and maintain exact basket proportions to preserve arbitrage profitability, because execution slippage or incomplete position closure will erode expected returns.
entry_conditions: Mispriced synthetic relationship identified between index option and stock basket
100% of required stock positions can be pre-positioned
MOC liquidity confirmed for all basket components
exit_conditions: All basket components closed at exchange MOC price
Any component fails MOC execution → liquidate entire position immediately

rule_text: When constructing multi-leg spreads with instruments of unequal point values, a trader must adjust position sizes to equalize dollar exposure across legs, not just notional points, to prevent unintended directional bias and losses from differential performance.
entry_conditions: Dollar value of long leg equals dollar value of short leg (within ±1%)
Point value ratios calculated and verified before position entry
Position size ratio = (Point_Value_Short / Point_Value_Long) × (Quantity_Target)
exit_conditions: Exit entire spread if dollar exposure drifts beyond ±2% due to price movement
Rebalance legs if tracking error exceeds tolerance

rule_text: When constructing multi-leg spreads with instruments of unequal point values, a trader must adjust position sizes to equalize dollar exposure across legs, not just notional points, to prevent unintended directional bias and losses from differential performance.

rule_text: A trader should establish a delta-neutral hedge between two correlated underlying instruments when comparing option theoretical value to actual price, so that profits come from relative strength differences rather than directional moves.
entry_conditions: ABC option mispricing > 2% vs theoretical value
Delta offset between ABC and XYZ positions = 99-101% (near-perfect neutrality)
Correlation between ABC and XYZ > 0.85
Both instruments have sufficient liquidity for hedging
exit_conditions: Delta drift exceeds ±2% of position notional
Mispricing converges within 0.5% of theoretical
Correlation falls below 0.80

rule_text: A trader should establish a short volatility spread (short straddle structure) when Greeks indicate negative gamma and vega with positive theta, signaling a belief that implied volatilities across markets are too high and will decline or mean-revert.
entry_conditions: Gamma < -500 (strong negative convexity)
Vega < -1200 (significant negative vega exposure)
Theta > +100 (substantial time decay benefit)
Implied volatility percentile > 80th (very elevated)
IV rank or IV percentile confirms top quartile volatility environment
exit_conditions: IV falls below 50th percentile OR
Underlying moves >2 standard deviations AND position loss exceeds 15% of spread debit/credit OR
Theta decay target met (50% of max theta captured)

source_citation: Option Volatility and Pricing, Natenberg, Ch.16, pp.358-359
rule_text: A trader should establish a short volatility spread (short straddle structure) when Greeks indicate negative gamma and vega with positive theta, signaling a belief that implied volatilities across markets are too high and will decline or mean-revert.
entry_conditions: Gamma < -300 (meaningful negative gamma)
Vega < -700 (moderate negative vega)
Theta > +50 (positive time decay)
Implied volatility percentile > 60th (above median)
Current IV > 20-day historical volatility moving average
exit_conditions: IV falls below 40th percentile OR
Underlying moves >1.5 standard deviations AND position loss exceeds 20% of risk capital allocated OR
Calendar expiration milestone reached (theta harvested for intended period)

source_citation: Option Volatility and Pricing, Natenberg, Ch.16, pp.358-359
rule_text: A trader should establish a short volatility spread (short straddle structure) when Greeks indicate negative gamma and vega with positive theta, signaling a belief that implied volatilities across markets are too high and will decline or mean-revert.
entry_conditions: Gamma < -150 (any negative convexity structure)
Vega < -400 (mild negative vega acceptable)
Theta > +25 (any positive theta contribution)
Implied volatility percentile > 40th (above median acceptable)
IV > realized volatility or forward-looking realized vol estimate
exit_conditions: IV falls below 25th percentile OR
Underlying moves >1 standard deviation AND position loss exceeds 25% of allocated risk OR
Time decay captured (exit after 50% of expected theta realized)

rule_text: A trader with a negative gamma position should actively rebalance delta exposure when the underlying moves significantly, because large moves will erode the position's value regardless of direction.

rule_text: In a ratio call spread, actively manage delta exposure by monitoring implied volatility changes across price levels, since falling volatility makes the position delta-positive and rising volatility makes it delta-negative.
entry_conditions: Establish ratio call spread (long OTM calls, short more ATM calls)
Record implied volatility at entry (baseline IV)
Set delta monitoring threshold at ±5 delta points from neutral (45-55 range)
exit_conditions: Exit or hedge when delta exceeds ±5 delta points from 50
Exit when IV falls more than 2% below entry IV (excessive positive delta)
Exit when IV rises more than 2% above entry IV (excessive negative delta)

rule_text: A trader should monitor and rebalance delta exposure when volatility changes, because rising volatility extends delta towards 50 (creating long exposure) while falling volatility pushes delta away from 50 (creating short exposure), both of which distort the intended position structure.

Volatility level recorded at entry
Calculate target delta range: ±8 ticks from entry delta
exit_conditions: Execute rebalance trade when delta moves beyond ±8 from entry
Re-measure and reset tolerance bands after rebalance

Cumulative theta decay > 50% of initial position profit buffer

rule_text: A trader should compare their presumed delta (based on own volatility assumptions) against implied delta (market-derived) and adjust hedging decisions when the divergence is material, acknowledging that volatility assumption uncertainty makes delta calculations inherently unreliable.

Unfortunately, this assumption seems to fly in the face of most traders’ experience.

when rising or
rule_text: Volatility is directionally dependent—rising markets in certain asset classes (agricultural, precious metals) exhibit higher volatility than falling markets, while falling markets in others (equities, bonds) exhibit higher volatility than rising markets. A trader should adjust volatility expectations and option pricing models based on the directional bias of the underlying market.
entry_conditions: Asset class identified as commodity (agricultural or precious metal)
Market in uptrend (price > 20-period MA)
Historical volatility shows 30%+ higher vol during rising periods
exit_conditions: Price closes below 20-period MA (trend reversal)
Realized vol reverts to baseline within 5%

rule_text: A trader should exploit the marketplace's relative mispricing of different strike prices by incorporating observed volatility skew into their theoretical pricing model to identify undervalued and overvalued options across the strike spectrum.
entry_conditions: Calculate implied volatility across strike prices
Measure skew magnitude as IV spread between 1-SD OTM and ATM: (IV_put_1SD - IV_ATM) > 8 percentage points
Compare trader's theoretical skew model to market skew
Entry only when market skew exceeds model skew by >1.5 standard deviations
exit_conditions: Close when skew converges to theoretical model within 0.5 std dev
Exit on calendar decay or 15% loss on position

rule_text: A trader should prioritize developing skill in predicting volatility/speed of the underlying market before entering options trading, because options strategies can profit from speed alone independent of direction, whereas directional prediction alone is insufficient for options success.

Trader has explicitly documented volatility edge vs market-implied volatility
exit_conditions: Accuracy falls below 65% over rolling 20-trade window

Time decay erodes edge below 4%

rule_text: When theoretical option value diverges significantly from market price, systematically isolate which model input (volatility, underlying price, interest rate, or dividends) explains the discrepancy, as fixed inputs (time, strike) can be eliminated as causes.

rule_text: A trader should exploit intra-market spreads when the price differential between delivery months diverges from the cost of carrying (financing and storage) the physical commodity between those months.
entry_conditions: Calculate theoretical carry cost = (Interest Rate × Time / 12 × Spot Price) + Storage/Insurance Costs
Actual spread price deviates from theoretical carry cost by >= 2.0%
Sufficient liquidity in both delivery months (bid-ask spread < 0.5% of price)
exit_conditions: Spread converges to within 0.5% of theoretical carry cost
Time decay erodes edge (exit 2 weeks before delivery)

rule_text: A trader should normalize volatility spreads by the absolute level of implied volatility before executing spread strategies, because the same percentage-point spread carries different trading significance depending on whether volatilities are low or high.
entry_conditions: Identify two legs with implied volatility mismatch
Calculate normalized spread: (IV_leg1 - IV_leg2) / ((IV_leg1 + IV_leg2) / 2)
Enter only if normalized spread > 15%
exit_conditions: Exit when normalized spread compresses below 5%
Exit when absolute IV level doubles or halves relative to entry

rule_text: A trader should initiate a backspread when expecting directional movement (and willing to accept credit-based financing), because backspreads profit from movement away from the long strike but lose when the underlying remains static.
entry_conditions: Implied volatility present at any level (no percentile requirement)
Net credit received ≥ 5% of max profit potential
Expected move is positive (IV-based move > 0)
Entry agnostic to directional bias; structure call or put based on liquidity
exit_conditions: Exit at 75% of max profit if achieved
Hold for directional move with no intermediate exits
Close at 7 DTE to avoid gamma risk on short options

rule_text: A trader should match straddle strategy selection (long vs. short) to expected market behavior: use long straddles when expecting significant directional movement, and short straddles when expecting the market to remain near the strike price, while being aware of the psychological difficulty of managing small losses in non-performing positions.

Expected absolute price move > 2.0 × recent historical volatility
Time to expiration ≥ 30 days
Confirmed directional catalyst or volatility expansion signal
exit_conditions: Exit at 50% max profit OR 21 days to expiration (whichever first)
Exit if catalyst fails to materialize after 5 days

rule_text: A trader should pay a price for a butterfly spread proportional to the probability-weighted likelihood of the underlying finishing at the inside strike at expiration, ranging from zero (low probability) to the max spread width (high probability).

Implied volatility suggests >70% probability of finishing within inner strikes
Expected move (1 SD) centers near inside strike
exit_conditions: Probability of center finish drops below 60%
Underlying moves >1.5 SD from entry
Time decay insufficient to justify holding

Time to expiration of long-term leg is 90+ days

source_citation: Option Volatility and Pricing, Natenberg, Ch. 8, pp. 166–167
rule_text: A trader should avoid or actively hedge time spread positions when borrowing constraints exist or corporate actions (tender offers, stock calls) create forced assignment risk before the spread's intended expiration cycle completes.
entry_conditions: Long calendar/time spread established (long-dated call/put, short near-dated)
Stock borrow rate increases OR borrow availability drops below 100%
Corporate action announced (tender offer, special dividend, merger)
exit_conditions: Close entire spread at market upon any borrow constraint detection
Exit upon any M&A or corporate action announcement affecting underlying stock

source_citation: Option Volatility and Pricing, Natenberg, Ch. 8, pp. 166–167
rule_text: A trader should avoid or actively hedge time spread positions when borrowing constraints exist or corporate actions (tender offers, stock calls) create forced assignment risk before the spread's intended expiration cycle completes.
entry_conditions: Long calendar/time spread in place
Stock borrow rate increases 50+ bps OR announced borrow shortage >50% of shares
Corporate action rumor or formal announcement of potential tender/call
exit_conditions: Buy back short-dated leg (eliminate assignment exposure) if borrow constraints persist
Reduce position size by 50% upon credible corporate action rumor

source_citation: Option Volatility and Pricing, Natenberg, Ch. 8, pp. 166–167
rule_text: A trader should avoid or actively hedge time spread positions when borrowing constraints exist or corporate actions (tender offers, stock calls) create forced assignment risk before the spread's intended expiration cycle completes.
entry_conditions: Long calendar/time spread established
Monitor borrow rates and corporate action probability
Continue position unless borrowing becomes impossible (availability <10%) or assignment is certain
exit_conditions: Close short-dated leg only in final 2 weeks if borrowing severely constrained
Exit only if forced assignment appears certain (e.g., tender offer accepted, exercise notice issued)

rule_text: A trader should exit or unwind a calendar spread position before assignment when the underlying approaches the strike price and stock lending becomes unavailable, because forced exercise or forced stock purchase at unfavorable prices will eliminate the remaining time value benefit.
entry_conditions: Long calendar spread position (long far-dated call, short near-dated call at same strike)
Underlying price within 2% of strike price (e.g., strike 70, underlying between 68.60 and 71.40)
Near-term call has less than 14 days to expiration
exit_conditions: Close entire spread at market if underlying breaches proximity threshold
Roll far-dated call to later expiration if underlying approaches strike

rule_text: A trader should exit or unwind a calendar spread position before assignment when the underlying approaches the strike price and stock lending becomes unavailable, because forced exercise or forced stock purchase at unfavorable prices will eliminate the remaining time value benefit.
entry_conditions: Long calendar spread position (long far-dated call, short near-dated call at same strike)
Underlying price within 4% of strike price (e.g., strike 70, underlying between 67.20 and 72.80)
Near-term call has less than 7 days to expiration
exit_conditions: Close spread if underlying moves through threshold
Roll if time value remaining exceeds cost of roll

Spreads which are helped by a rise in volatility have a positive vega. These include

rule_text: Match spread type to volatility directional outlook: deploy positive vega spreads when expecting volatility rise, and negative vega spreads when expecting volatility decline.
entry_conditions: IV percentile >= 90 for negative vega spreads (short straddles, ratio verticals, long butterflies)
IV percentile <= 10 for positive vega spreads (long straddles, backspreads, long strangles)
Volatility trend confirmed over 20+ days
Position size limited to 1-2% of account per spread
exit_conditions: IV percentile moves to 50th percentile
Time decay exceeds expected value accrual
Stop loss at 2x initial credit/debit

rule_text: A trader should select a volatility spread structure (Backspread, Ratio Vertical, Long Time, or Short Time) that matches their directional view on implied volatility and gamma exposure, because each spread category has a defined gamma-vega sign combination that determines how it profits or loses under different volatility and price movement scenarios.
entry_conditions: IV percentile rank >= 75th (high conviction IV forecast)
ATM straddle IV rank >= 70%
Expected realized volatility > implied volatility by >= 5 percentage points
Spread Greeks align with BOTH IV and gamma forecasts
exit_conditions: IV converges to forecast within 2 percentage points
Realized volatility deviation from expectation exceeds 3 percentage points
Time decay loss exceeds 10% of position notional

rule_text: A delta-neutral trader should classify and size their position intensity based on the magnitude of gamma and vega, where larger absolute values of these Greeks indicate stronger commitment to that directional Greek exposure (gamma for directional moves, vega for volatility moves).

Ratio of vega to gamma magnitude is consistent with stated strategy
exit_conditions: Delta drifts beyond ±15

Position thesis invalidated by market move

rule_text: When all options are uniformly mispriced (all cheap or all expensive), avoid simple straddles/strangles despite positive theoretical edge; instead use backspread or ratio vertical spreads that accept some overvalued/undervalued options to reduce tail risk.
entry_conditions: All options in chain are >15% mispriced in same direction (all cheap OR all expensive)
Straddle theoretical edge would be positive
Backspread/ratio spread alternative exists with ≤30% overvalued component
exit_conditions: Mispricing converges toward fair value
Overvalued leg reaches cost basis loss limit

rule_text: A trader should construct long time spreads when implied volatility is low and expected to rise, and short time spreads when implied volatility is high and expected to fall, but may deviate from these guidelines if they predict implied volatility will not correlate with underlying volatility changes.

IV trend indicator shows rising momentum (e.g., 5-day IV slope > 0)
Historical volatility < Implied Volatility by at least 2 standard deviations
No major event risk in front month
exit_conditions: IV rises to 50th percentile
Time decay erosion exceeds 15% of initial credit
Underlying moves beyond 1 standard deviation

rule_text: A trader should be more concerned about underestimating volatility than overestimating it, because volatility underestimation creates asymmetric risk exposure where losses from adverse vol moves exceed gains from favorable moves in certain spread structures.
entry_conditions: Volatility estimate is set 2+ percentile points above realized historical volatility
Spread's P&L asymmetry ratio (loss if vol is 3pp lower / gain if vol is 3pp higher) is ≤0.5
Expected value of spread under vol uncertainty >0 even if vol is 2pp lower than estimate
exit_conditions: Realized volatility moves beyond initial estimate range by >2pp
Spread P&L loss accumulates to 50% of max risk

Margin for error exists in at least 2 major risk categories (primary Greeks)
Maximum loss on spread is ≤2% of total account capital
Spread has cushion of ±1.5 standard deviations across Greeks
exit_conditions: Exit if any single risk parameter moves beyond ±1 SD from entry
Exit if cumulative unrealized loss reaches 75% of allocated risk capital for this trade

rule_text: A trader should only enter illiquid option spreads if willing to hold the position through expiration, scaling position size inversely with liquidity and time-to-expiration.
entry_conditions: Bid-ask spread < 1% of mid price OR reject entry
Days to expiration < 90 days OR contract size ≤ 10% of portfolio
Trader confirms willingness to hold until expiration in writing
exit_conditions: Exit only at fair value or better; do not force exit due to liquidity stress

We know that a straddle becomes more valuable as the underlying price moves
away from the exercise price, resulting in one side of the straddle, either the call or put,
going more deeply into-the-mone
rule_text: A trader should buy the relatively underpriced straddle and sell the relatively overpriced straddle when two straddles at different strikes show a mispricing relative to their theoretical moneyness distance from the underlying price.
entry_conditions: Identify two straddles at different strikes
Calculate price differential as percentage of ATM straddle value
Enter when further OTM straddle is 3-4% cheaper than nearer ITM straddle
Confirm moneyness gap is at least $2.50 between strikes
exit_conditions: Close when price differential narrows to 1%
Stop loss if differential widens beyond 6%
Time-based exit 7 days before expiration

source_citation: Option Volatility and Pricing, Natenberg, Ch. 11, pp. 245-246
raw_chunk_text:
We know that a straddle becomes more valuable as the underlying price moves

rule_text: A trader should buy the relatively underpriced straddle and sell the relatively overpriced straddle when two straddles at different strikes show a mispricing relative to their theoretical moneyness distance from the underlying price.
entry_conditions: Identify two straddles at different strikes
Calculate price differential as percentage of ATM straddle value
Enter when further OTM straddle is 2% or more cheaper than nearer ITM straddle
Moneyness gap can be as small as $1.00 between strikes
exit_conditions: Close when price differential narrows to 0.5%
Stop loss if differential widens beyond 4%
Time-based exit 5 days before expiration

source_citation: Option Volatility and Pricing, Natenberg, Ch. 240, pp. 249-250
raw_chunk_text:
Early Exercise
of American Options
Thus far we have assumed that all option strategies involve holding a position to
expiration. Since the great majority of exchange traded options are American, carry
rule_text: A trader should exercise an American option early when the present value of immediate cash flow from exercise exceeds the time value remaining in the option.
entry_conditions: Immediate cash flow (intrinsic + dividends) calculated
Remaining time value estimated using Black-Scholes or binomial model
Cash flow advantage > 1.5x time value remaining
exit_conditions: Exercise immediately upon condition met
Do not hold for potential further appreciation

exit_conditions: Exercise when both ratio and time conditions satisfied

rule_text: A trader should exercise an in-the-money call option (converting it to a futures position) when the interest earned on the credited account balance over the remaining time to expiration exceeds the time value remaining in the option.
entry_conditions: Call option is in-the-money by at least 2 points
Interest earned on settlement credit ≥ 1.5× remaining time value
Time to expiration ≤ 60 days
Risk-free rate ≥ 4%
exit_conditions: Do not exercise if time value > calculated interest benefit

rule_text: When an imminent dividend payment equals or exceeds the remaining time value of a long call option, exercise the option to capture the dividend rather than hold the option, since the dividend offsets the loss from early exercise.
entry_conditions: Long call option position exists
Stock is about to go ex-dividend
Dividend payment ≥ 1.5x the option's remaining time value
Intrinsic value + dividend ≥ exercise cost relative to current stock price
exit_conditions: Exercise the call option
Receive dividend as stock holder
Close position or hold stock

rule_text: A trader should hold an American put option through the ex-dividend date and exercise it early when interest rate considerations are sufficiently favorable, because dividend avoidance and interest savings create early exercise value that European puts cannot capture.
entry_conditions: American put purchased in-the-money or near-the-money
Holding period extends through upcoming ex-dividend date
Annualized interest cost on stock purchase > 2.0% of strike
exit_conditions: Ex-dividend date passed
Interest savings + dividend avoidance > 2.0% of strike value
Put drops below parity by more than 0.05

Time to expiration ≥ 30 days

Early assignment occurs
Days to expiration < 5

rule_text: A trader should select volatility strategies by comparing historical volatility over the time-to-expiration horizon against its long-term mean, trend, and implied volatility level to match strategy duration to volatility regime.
entry_conditions: Historical volatility (HV) measured over time-to-expiration window ≥ 1.5× long-term mean volatility
Implied volatility trend is downward or flat
Volatility stability metric (e.g., rolling std dev of HV) is below 50th percentile of historical range
Duration of options ≤ 50% of measured lookback period
exit_conditions: HV reverts to within 1.1× mean
IV trend reverses to upward
Volatility stability increases above 65th percentile

Historical volatility declining or stable (not trending up sharply)
IV term structure slope > 0.5 vol points per month difference
exit_conditions: Short-term IV falls to within 1.5 vol points of long-term IV
Historical volatility reverses and begins rising (regime shift)
Position P&L reaches +20% on time value capture

rule_text: A delta-neutral long volatility position profits from volatility expansion only if realized volatility (actual price movement) exceeds implied volatility over the holding period; time decay erodes gains from implied volatility increases when price remains static.
entry_conditions: Expected realized volatility >= Implied volatility + 3%
Holding period >= 30 days
Theta decay at entry <= 0.5% of position notional per day
Delta-hedge the long call position with short futures
exit_conditions: Realized volatility falls below IV by 1%
Time decay consumes 50% of vega gains
Position reaches 30-day horizon

rule_text: A trader should obtain precise dividend payout timing and amounts before initiating arbitrage strategies involving futures, as large dividend payouts can reverse expected profitability if not accurately accounted for in fair value calculations.

All dividend payouts for contract expiration known with certainty ≥ 30 days prior

rule_text: A trader executing a program trade should continuously monitor and rebalance delta exposure when interest rate changes alter the delta of futures contracts, to maintain intended delta neutrality or risk posture.
entry_conditions: Program trade (long stocks + short futures or vice versa) is established
Interest rate change occurs
Futures delta deviates from target by ≥10 basis points (e.g., from 100 to 99 or 101)
exit_conditions: Position is rebalanced to delta neutrality or target delta
Interest rate stability resumes for 24+ hours

Buy program only if dividend yield stable or rising; sell program only if dividend yield stable or falling
exit_conditions: Exit if any constituent announces dividend policy reversal
Exit if cumulative dividend uncertainty reaches 20% of constituents

rule_text: A trader should take positions in options rather than the underlying instrument when implied volatility diverges from their volatility forecast, buying options when IV is too low and selling when IV is too high.
entry_conditions: Implied Volatility < (Trader Volatility Forecast × 0.95)
OR Implied Volatility > (Trader Volatility Forecast × 1.05)
exit_conditions: IV converges to within 1% of forecast
Trade deteriorates by 0.5% of position value

rule_text: A trader should buy options (calls for long exposure) when implied volatility is lower than the trader's estimate of fair volatility, and sell options (or use them defensively) when implied volatility is higher than fair volatility, to gain a theoretical edge beyond the spread itself.
entry_conditions: Implied volatility < Trader's estimated fair volatility
Absolute difference >= 3.0% (e.g., IV at 20%, trader estimate 23%+)
Spread directional component also exists
exit_conditions: Implied volatility converges to within 1.0% of fair value estimate
Spread leg loses directional validity

rule_text: A trader should use options instead of underlying instruments when their directional conviction about relative strength between two assets conflicts with the implied volatility ranking, allowing them to profit from volatility repricing rather than betting on absolute price movement.
entry_conditions: Trader has directional conviction that Asset A will outperform Asset B
Implied volatility of Asset B exceeds implied volatility of Asset A by at least 600 basis points
Both assets have implied volatility levels between 15% and 40%
exit_conditions: Implied volatility spread narrows to 300 bps or less
Position time decay exceeds expected volatility realization benefit
Conviction in relative strength reverses

Construct spread using only strikes meeting ATM criteria

exit_conditions: Close when spread moves outside delta band [0.48, 0.52]
Exit if underlying moves beyond 1% moneyness constraint

rule_text: A trader should construct delta-neutral spreads weighted by individual option deltas when expecting relative outperformance of one underlying versus another, positioning to profit from the anticipated relative movement while remaining neutral to absolute market direction.

Delta ratio between legs within 10% of calculated neutral ratio
Spread net debit/credit acceptable within 5% of short option premium received
exit_conditions: Relative outperformance thesis invalidated
Position reaches 50% max profit
30 days to expiration

rule_text: A trader should construct a volatility spread (long low-IV, short high-IV) when implied volatilities diverge between two related underlyings, positioning for the higher-volatility-priced instrument to underperform the lower-volatility-priced instrument.

Historical correlation between underlyings ≥ 0.70
No strong directional bias in either underlying (neutral technical setup)
Sufficient liquidity in both option legs to establish position at reasonable spreads
exit_conditions: IV spread contracts to ≤ 2%
Profit target hit (40% of max profit)
Stop loss hit (80% of max loss)
Correlation breaks below 0.60

rule_text: A trader should construct a ratio spread between two correlated underlyings when expecting differential rate of movement, selling the option on the stronger underlying and buying the option on the weaker underlying to profit from relative volatility and directional divergence.
entry_conditions: Identify two underlyings with historical correlation >= 0.85
Implied volatility spread (long IV - short IV) >= 3 percentage points
Conviction that weaker underlying will move down at least 1.25x faster than stronger
At-the-money or near-the-money strikes only
exit_conditions: Exit when correlation drops below 0.80
Exit at 50% of max theoretical profit
Exit when relative move assumption breaks (weaker underlying moves slower than 1.15x stronger)

rule_text: A trader should construct relative volatility spreads using at-the-money straddles sized proportionally to isolate volatility exposure and eliminate directional bias when comparing two correlated markets.
entry_conditions: Two correlated markets identified (e.g., ABC and XYZ)
Volatility skew/term structure materially different between markets
Both markets have liquid ATM straddle options
Delta sum across both legs equals zero (within 1 delta tolerance)
exit_conditions: Realized volatility spread converges to implied spread
Correlation between markets breaks down
Max loss threshold hit (2% of capital per spread)

source_citation: Option Volatility and Pricing, Natenberg, Ch.16, pp.350-351
raw_chunk_text:
Since predicting the correct volatility of even one underlying contract can be such a
difficult task, what chance does the trader have of being correct about the volatility of
two different contracts?
rule_text: In intermarket spreads, focus on the volatility relationship (ratio) between contracts rather than predicting absolute volatility levels, since the spread value depends on maintaining a consistent volatility ratio.
entry_conditions: Volatility ratio between contract A and contract B calculated over 252-day lookback period
Historical standard deviation of ratio < 2%
Current ratio within ±1 standard deviation of mean ratio
Ratio stability confirmed across 3+ separate market regimes
exit_conditions: Volatility ratio moves beyond ±2 standard deviations from mean
Historical ratio relationship breaks (std dev of ratio exceeds 3%)

source_citation: Option Volatility and Pricing, Natenberg, Ch.16, pp.350-351
raw_chunk_text:
Since predicting the correct volatility of even one underlying contract can be such a
difficult task, what chance does the trader have of being correct about the volatility of
two different contracts?
rule_text: In intermarket spreads, focus on the volatility relationship (ratio) between contracts rather than predicting absolute volatility levels, since the spread value depends on maintaining a consistent volatility ratio.
entry_conditions: Volatility ratio between contract A and contract B calculated over 126-day lookback period
Historical standard deviation of ratio < 3.5%
Current ratio within ±1.5 standard deviations of mean ratio
Ratio stability confirmed across 2 separate market regimes
exit_conditions: Volatility ratio moves beyond ±1.5 standard deviations from mean
Historical ratio relationship degrades (std dev of ratio exceeds 4.5%)

If it is dangerous to sell at-the-money options going into expiration, perhaps there is

rule_text: A trader should buy at-the-money straddles as expiration approaches when anticipating potential market gaps, because the asymmetric risk/reward (limited theta loss vs. unlimited gamma gain) is preferable to selling, which carries high negative gamma risk despite positive theta.
entry_conditions: Days to expiration ≤ 3
Moneyness within 0.95-1.05 (ATM)
Implied volatility percentile ≥ 75th
Recent gap events in underlying OR earnings/event announcement within 5 days
exit_conditions: Expiration reached
Underlying moves >2 standard deviations
Theta decay exceeds unrealized P&L gain

rule_text: A trader should investigate and potentially arbitrage or rebalance when implied volatilities across different strike prices for the same underlying and expiration diverge significantly, as only one true volatility should exist for a given contract over its remaining life.

Time to expiration allows for execution and convergence

Time decay reduces position value to negligible amount
Market liquidity deteriorates at entry strikes

source_citation: Option Volatility and Pricing, Natenberg, Ch. 4, pp. 78-79
raw_chunk_text:
VOLATILITY AND OBSERVED PRICE CHANGES
Why is it important for a trader to be able to estimate daily or weekly price changes from
an annual volatility? Volatility is the one input into a theoretical pr
rule_text: A trader should validate that the implied volatility input used in pricing models is consistent with observed daily/weekly price changes, rather than relying solely on theoretical prices.
entry_conditions: Calculate 30-day realized volatility from observed daily price changes
Compare realized vol to implied vol assumption used in pricing model
Trade only when absolute difference is less than 1.5 percentage points
exit_conditions: Exit if realized volatility diverges from assumption by more than 2.5 percentage points
Exit if model assumptions are invalidated by market data

rule_text: A trader should reject or re-evaluate option prices when the quoted price is stale (not recent) and underlying market conditions have changed materially, as this creates significant discrepancies between quoted and true implied volatility.

Implied volatility at stale price differs from current IV by > 1.0 percentage point
exit_conditions: Do not trade; wait for fresh quote
Or recalculate fair value using current underlying price and IV

rule_text: A trader should construct a delta-neutral hedge by offsetting the delta position of an options contract with an equal and opposite delta position in futures, such that the sum of all deltas equals zero.

rule_text: A trader managing a hedged option position should expect profit from rebalancing adjustments when realized volatility exceeds the implied volatility priced into the option; conversely, profit diminishes or turns to loss when realized volatility falls below implied volatility.
entry_conditions: Realized volatility (10-day rolling) > Implied volatility × 1.05
Implied volatility is in lower quartile of historical range
Liquidity is sufficient for frequent rebalancing (bid-ask spread < 2% of option value)
exit_conditions: Realized volatility equals or falls below Implied volatility
Implied volatility spikes above realized volatility
Any daily loss exceeds 5% of position value

rule_text: A trader should close a hedge early when implied volatility has risen sufficiently to lock in a profit on the option leg, rather than holding until expiration.

Option leg has gained 15% or more of its initial premium value
exit_conditions: Close entire hedge (exit both option and futures legs simultaneously)
Lock in profit from IV expansion on option leg

rule_text: A trader should exploit mispricings between an option's market price and its theoretical value by establishing a dynamic delta-hedge position, capturing profit equal to the pricing discrepancy regardless of subsequent price movement.
entry_conditions: Option market price deviates from theoretical value by at least 3%
Implied volatility is within 10% of realized volatility estimate
Time to expiration is at least 14 days
Bid-ask spread is less than 2% of option premium
exit_conditions: Mispricing has been captured (profit target reached)
Realized volatility diverges >15% from initial estimate
Position delta drift exceeds 20% of initial delta
Time decay has consumed >50% of remaining time value

Foreign rate approaches domestic rate

rule_text: A trader should only enter an option position when the position has a positive theoretical edge—the sum of (theoretical value minus trade price) × contracts across all legs must be positive.

No individual leg has negative edge exceeding -0.5% of that leg's notional value
exit_conditions: Exit if theoretical edge becomes negative
Exit if position Greeks (delta, gamma, vega, theta) move materially against directional assumption

rule_text: A trader should hedge a directional option position using theoretically equivalent instruments (other options or futures) rather than only the underlying, to isolate and profit from volatility mispricings while neutralizing directional risk.

Underlying instrument is liquid and tradeable with tight spreads
exit_conditions: Mispricing closes (option reprices to theoretical value)

Days to expiration ≥ 7 days
exit_conditions: Exit at 50% of max profit

Exit at expiration or 3 days before

rule_text: A trader should select time spread direction (long vs. short) based on expected volatility regime and underlying price movement expectations, because time spreads respond oppositely to volatility and directional moves compared to other spreads.
entry_conditions: For SHORT time spread: Historical volatility > 60th percentile AND implied volatility declining for 3+ consecutive periods
For LONG time spread: IV term structure slope > +2% AND realized volatility < 40th percentile AND price in range-bound consolidation (ATR < 0.5 * 20-day average)
exit_conditions: Short time spread: Exit if IV rises back above entry level or underlying consolidates
Long time spread: Exit if IV term structure inverts or realized volatility spikes above 60th percentile

rule_text: A trader should value a call time spread (calendar spread) with a minimum floor equal to the cost of carry on the underlying stock, even when both options are deeply in-the-money and volatility is very low.
entry_conditions: Both call options are deeply in-the-money (delta > 0.90)
Implied volatility is very low (IV < 20th percentile of historical range)
Calculated spread value < 125% of annualized cost of carry
exit_conditions: Spread value reaches 125% of cost of carry
Volatility increases above 30th percentile

rule_text: A trader should size positions and manage capital allocation to survive inevitable periods of adverse results, ensuring that short-term losses cannot terminate the trading career or force liquidation.
entry_conditions: Portfolio equity drawdown tolerance < 20%
Maximum position notional exposure < 5% of total capital
Minimum cash reserve ratio > 30% of capital

Reduce position size if drawdown reaches 10%

Bid/ask spread widens beyond initial threshold during execution

rule_text: A trader should construct synthetic positions (long or short) by combining opposite options at the same strike and expiration to replicate underlying instrument exposure while deferring actual ownership until expiration.

Allow natural exercise/assignment at expiration

rule_text: When two contracts are economically equivalent but trading at different prices, execute an arbitrage by buying the cheaper contract and selling the more expensive one to capture the mispricing.
entry_conditions: Calculate synthetic price (call − put + strike) and real underlying price
Compute absolute price difference between real and synthetic
Verify difference exceeds 0.75% of underlying contract price
Confirm both legs are liquid with tight bid-ask spreads
exit_conditions: Arbitrage closes when synthetic and real prices converge
Exit if spread widens beyond 0.50% during holding period

rule_text: Execute a conversion arbitrage by simultaneously buying the underpriced instrument and selling the overpriced synthetic equivalent when the two trading vehicles are economically equivalent but priced differently.
entry_conditions: Synthetic price - underlying price >= 0.75% of underlying notional value

Remaining time to expiration >= 30 days
exit_conditions: Exit at expiration
Close if mispricing reverts by 50% of entry spread within 5 trading days

rule_text: Execute a reverse conversion (buy synthetic, sell underlying futures) when the synthetic spread price is sufficiently below the underlying futures price to lock in an arbitrage profit after transaction costs.
entry_conditions: Calculate synthetic price = call_price + put_price - underlying_futures_price
Measure spread differential = underlying_futures_price - synthetic_price
Entry trigger: spread differential >= 0.25 (250 ticks or basis points depending on contract)
exit_conditions: Position automatically resolves at synthetic maturity/expiration
Close if profit target exceeded (lock in gains if spread widens further)

rule_text: A trader should account for financing costs (carrying costs) when executing synthetic arbitrage reversals, as these costs erode expected breakeven returns and must be factored into the profitability threshold.
entry_conditions: Identify synthetic mispricing (call - put ≠ futures - strike)
Calculate carrying cost = debit × interest_rate × time_fraction
Verify: observed_mispricing > 3.0 × carrying_cost
exit_conditions: Exit at expiration or when carrying cost accumulation exceeds 50% of captured spread

rule_text: When executing a conversion arbitrage, offset the cost of carrying the debit position to expiration by adjusting one or more legs of the trade (call purchase price, put sale price, or futures sale price) by an amount equal to the carrying cost.
entry_conditions: Identify conversion arbitrage opportunity with calculable debit balance
Calculate carrying cost = debit_balance × interest_rate × time_to_expiration
Adjust only the futures leg by full carrying cost amount (sell futures at higher price)
exit_conditions: Exit at expiration when conversion arbitrage resolves
Exit if market prices move and no longer cover carrying cost

rule_text: A trader should account for interest rate risk on cash flows in conversion and reversal strategies, as the cost of carrying a debit or earnings on a credit materially affects trade value, even in nominally 'riskless' arbitrage strategies.

Arbitrage profit margin (after carrying costs) > 2.0% annualized
exit_conditions: Margin shrinks below 1.0% due to rate changes
Interest rates rise more than 50 bps since entry
Holding period exceeds 30 days

rule_text: A trader should ensure sufficient capital reserves to maintain synthetic positions through adverse price moves, because forced liquidation due to margin/funding constraints will always result in losses regardless of the underlying position's theoretical profitability.
entry_conditions: Synthetic position (short call + long put) is established
Calculate maximum adverse move: futures move ≥ 2 standard deviations from entry
Calculate funding requirement = max adverse move × contract multiplier × position size
Available liquid capital ≥ 150% × funding requirement
exit_conditions: Exit entire position if liquid capital falls below 150% of current margin requirement
Exit if funding requirement grows beyond available capital

rule_text: A trader should construct box spreads by simultaneously executing both legs (call vertical and put vertical) when both can be traded at prices that collectively offer arbitrage value relative to the box's theoretical value.

Sum the two leg entry prices
Calculate deviation: |theoretical_value - combined_entry_price|
Execute only if deviation >= 0.50 points
exit_conditions: Close both legs when box converges to intrinsic value (expiration)
Unwind entire position if either leg becomes illiquid before both fills confirmed

rule_text: A trader can neutralize directional exposure from an initial position by executing an offsetting synthetic position in a different expiration month at the same strike, creating a calendar spread that isolates time decay and volatility dynamics between expirations.
entry_conditions: Days to near-term expiration < 14 days
Implied volatility difference (far - near) > 3 percentage points
Original directional position fully identified and marked
Strike selection at ATM or 1 strike OTM
exit_conditions: Exit when calendar spread reaches 50% of max theoretical profit
Exit if underlying moves >2 standard deviations from entry strike

rule_text: A trader should construct a ratio spread with a target net credit by working backwards from acceptable bid/ask prices, ensuring that the credit received from short legs minus the cost of long legs equals the desired credit target.
entry_conditions: Identify target net credit (e.g., 1.00 point)
Wait for short leg (ATM call) to trade at or better than ask price
Calculate required long leg bid price: (short_leg_ask_price - target_credit) / ratio_multiplier
Only enter if actual long leg (OTM call) bid price ≤ calculated requirement
exit_conditions: Exit when net credit target is achieved with 20-30% margin of safety
Exit if spread widens beyond 0.15 points from target

rule_text: A trader should construct a ratio spread with a target net credit by working backwards from acceptable bid/ask prices, ensuring that the credit received from short legs minus the cost of long legs equals the desired credit target.
entry_conditions: Identify target net credit (e.g., 1.00 point)
Monitor both directions: (short_ask - 2×long_bid) and (short_bid - 2×long_ask)
Enter when either calculation yields net credit within target ± 0.05 points
Accept fills from whichever direction triggers first
exit_conditions: Exit when net credit target achieved with 10-15% margin of safety
Exit if spread widens beyond 0.20 points from target

rule_text: When vertical spreads (call or put) violate their theoretical value ordering relative to strike prices, exploit the mispricing by executing an arbitrage trade in the direction that restores equilibrium.

Time decay reduces remaining edge below transaction costs
Holding to expiration if spread remains profitable

rule_text: A trader should compare butterfly spreads across different expirations to identify relative mispricing, recognizing that longer-dated butterflies may trade at lower absolute prices despite having more time value, creating potential arbitrage or relative value opportunities.

exit_conditions: Exit when ratio reverts to >0.85
Exit at expiration of near-term contract

rule_text: A trader should sell a deeply in-the-money American option and establish a synthetic position in the underlying when the option's bid/ask spread indicates it is trading near or above parity, rather than exercising or holding the option.

Calculate maximum assignment liability across all short option legs

Assignment occurs and cash settled

rule_text: A trader holding a voluntary long position should hedge downside risk using options or futures when expecting short-term decline, choosing the hedge method based on its cost relative to the cost of liquidating and re-entering the position.

Decline occurs and hedge profit offsets position loss
Risk environment improves (decline probability falls below 25%)

Primary objective is to eliminate or reverse hedge cost
Acceptable with reduced downside protection range
exit_conditions: Position liquidated or underlying contract closed
Called away at higher call strike (capped profit realization)
Net premium received through spread creation achieved

rule_text: A trader should construct a collar hedge by selling an out-of-the-money call and buying an out-of-the-money put such that the call premium exceeds the put premium, generating a net credit that reduces the effective cost basis of the underlying position.
entry_conditions: Long underlying position held
Sold call strike is 5% above current price
Bought put strike is 5% below current price
Net credit from spread ≥ 0.25% of underlying price
exit_conditions: Exit when underlying price touches put strike (downside protected)
Exit when underlying price touches call strike (upside capped)
Exit at position expiration

Position delta exceeds ±0.30 at any strike

Time decay reaches 50% of spread life

rule_text: When hedging a long position with a vertical call spread, adjust the strike selection based on implied volatility level: sell ATM and buy OTM when IV is high; sell ITM and buy ATM when IV is low. Always construct spreads with negative delta but positive theoretical edge by ensuring the sold leg is more volatility-sensitive than the bought leg.
entry_conditions: Long underlying position exists requiring protection
IV percentile >= 75th percentile for HIGH IV regime
IV percentile < 25th percentile for LOW IV regime
Theoretical edge of spread >= 1.5% of short strike value
exit_conditions: Position underlying closes, exit spread
Spread reaches 50% of max profit
Underlying moves more than 3 ATR beyond short strike

rule_text: When direct hedging instruments are unavailable or costly, a trader should replicate the target payoff structure through dynamic rebalancing of the underlying portfolio, adjusting exposure continuously to match the delta characteristics of the desired protective instrument.
entry_conditions: Desired put option is unavailable or bid-ask spread > 5% of premium
Portfolio notional value > $50M
Set floor protection level at 95% of current portfolio value
exit_conditions: Portfolio value exceeds floor + 10% cushion
Accumulated rebalancing costs exceed 3% of premium equivalent

rule_text: Volatility exhibits mean-reversion behavior—it fluctuates within a historical range and systematically reverses after extreme moves, unlike price which is open-ended. A trader should exploit volatility extremes by positioning for reversion toward the historical mean.
entry_conditions: Current volatility <= 10th percentile of 10-year historical range (5% for DM example)
OR Current volatility >= 90th percentile of 10-year historical range (20% for DM example)
Position only after 5+ days at extreme to confirm reversal has NOT begun
exit_conditions: Exit when volatility reverts to 50th percentile of historical range
Stop loss if volatility penetrates beyond 95th/5th percentile

rule_text: Volatility exhibits both mean-reverting and trending characteristics at different time horizons; traders should identify whether volatility is in a trending phase or reverting phase and position accordingly.
entry_conditions: Volatility has moved in one direction for minimum 20 trading days
Current volatility is at 75th percentile or higher (trending up) OR at 25th percentile or lower (trending down)
Minor counter-trend move of 5-10% reversal does not yet signal regime end
exit_conditions: Volatility reverses 15% from entry direction
Trend period expires after 40 trading days without new extremes

rule_text: A trader should blend implied volatility with historical volatility forecasts using dynamic weighting based on confidence in their own volatility prediction model.

exit_conditions: Confidence in volatility forecast drops below 70%

pants with respect to the expected amount of underlying price fluctuation over the
remaining life of an option.
rule_text: A trader should adjust their volatility expectations and position bias when realized historical volatility diverges from implied volatility, as the market consensus will eventually reprice toward realized volatility levels.
entry_conditions: IV is 1.5+ standard deviations above/below 20-day realized volatility
Divergence has persisted for at least 5 trading days
Current realized volatility is trending in direction that will compress the spread
exit_conditions: IV-to-HV spread returns to within 0.5 standard deviations
Position duration exceeds 30 calendar days
Realized volatility reverses sharply in opposite direction

if the futures price is 432.70, and we assume that it is trading at fair value, what should

rule_text: A trader should exploit index arbitrage opportunities when the futures price deviates from its theoretical fair value (calculated as spot price + dividends - carrying costs), with larger deviations presenting stronger trading signals.

Compute mispricing_percent = |futures_price - fair_value| / fair_value × 100
Enter when mispricing_percent ≥ 1.0%
exit_conditions: Exit when mispricing_percent ≤ 0.2%
Exit at expiration if trade remains open

rule_text: A trader should execute a synthetic arbitrage (buy underpriced call, sell overpriced put, sell underlying) when the call-put spread deviates from its theoretical synthetic relationship by a measurable margin.
entry_conditions: Calculate theoretical call price using put-call parity: C_theoretical = P + S - K*e^(-rT)
Measure actual call-put spread deviation: (C_actual - P_actual) - (C_theoretical - P_theoretical)
Enter only when absolute deviation exceeds 2.50 (250 bps equivalent)
exit_conditions: Close entire position when deviation narrows to 0.50 or less
Exit if underlying moves >1% before position converges

rule_text: A trader should substitute a highly correlated futures contract for the underlying cash basket in option strategies when European options with no early exercise feature are being arbitraged, because futures and the index will correlate perfectly and converge at expiration, enabling riskless arbitrage execution.
entry_conditions: Option style must be confirmed European (no early exercise)
Correlation between futures and underlying index >= 0.99
Days to expiration >= 30 days
Basis spread between futures and cash index <= 0.05%
exit_conditions: Correlation falls below 0.98
Basis spread widens beyond 0.10%
Early exercise feature emerges (switch to cash)

rule_text: A trader should execute cross-market arbitrage when a synthetic position and its corresponding futures contract are mispriced by the same magnitude relative to fair value, exploiting the price differential by buying the cheaper instrument and selling the more expensive one.
entry_conditions: Absolute mispricing between synthetic and futures >= 1.50 points
Mispricing observed in at least 3 index markets with correlation >= 0.95
Bid-ask spread in both legs combined < 30% of mispricing magnitude
Transaction costs (commissions + slippage) < 25% of theoretical profit
exit_conditions: Price convergence to fair value within 0.10 points
Either leg loses liquidity (bid-ask widens > 50%)
Mispricing reverses direction before closure

rule_text: A trader should construct intermarket spreads only between markets that exhibit strong relational similarity, driven by the same underlying forces, rather than between unrelated markets.
entry_conditions: Correlation coefficient between markets >= 0.80 (measured over 252-day rolling window)
Both markets must be in the same asset class or share identical commodity/currency drivers
Visual confirmation of synchronized price movements over multiple time periods
exit_conditions: Correlation falls below 0.75
Relationship breaks down with divergent price action
Fundamental driver for one market changes

One market experiences idiosyncratic shock unrelated to the other

rule_text: A trader holding a long gamma position must actively rebalance the delta hedge as the underlying price moves, and must account for theta decay which erodes the position value each day without price movement.
entry_conditions: Position has positive gamma (long options or short spread)

Stock price within range where gamma effect is largest
exit_conditions: Exit entire position if theta decay exceeds -0.15 per day with no offsetting gamma profit
Reduce size by 30% if position P&L deteriorates by theta alone over 3 consecutive days

source_citation: Option Volatility and Pricing, Natenberg, Ch. 17, pp. 368-369
rule_text: A trader should balance long and short call positions to control upside risk exposure, and balance long and short put positions to control downside risk exposure, because unequal positions create unlimited directional risk.
entry_conditions: Count long calls in position
Count short calls in position
Count long puts in position
Count short puts in position
Verify calls are perfectly balanced (long calls = short calls)
Verify puts are perfectly balanced (long puts = short puts)
exit_conditions: Exit immediately if long calls ≠ short calls
Exit immediately if long puts ≠ short puts

source_citation: Option Volatility and Pricing, Natenberg, Ch. 17, pp. 368-369
rule_text: A trader should balance long and short call positions to control upside risk exposure, and balance long and short put positions to control downside risk exposure, because unequal positions create unlimited directional risk.
entry_conditions: Count long calls vs short calls
Count long puts vs short puts
Calculate net call ratio: long_calls / (short_calls + 1)
Calculate net put ratio: long_puts / (short_puts + 1)
Enter when both ratios between 0.8 and 1.2 (within 20% imbalance)
exit_conditions: Exit when call imbalance exceeds 20%
Exit when put imbalance exceeds 20%

source_citation: Option Volatility and Pricing, Natenberg, Ch. 17, pp. 368-369
rule_text: A trader should balance long and short call positions to control upside risk exposure, and balance long and short put positions to control downside risk exposure, because unequal positions create unlimited directional risk.
entry_conditions: Identify directional bias (bullish or bearish)
If bullish: hold more long calls than short calls (up to 2:1 ratio)
If bearish: hold more long puts than short puts (up to 2:1 ratio)
Ensure opposite tail is balanced (if call-heavy, balance puts 1:1)
exit_conditions: Exit if directional ratio exceeds 2:1
Exit if opposite tail becomes imbalanced

rule_text: A trader should not rely solely on theoretical Greeks being neutral; instead, validate that the pricing model assumptions and input data are correct before treating a position as truly hedged.

Any Greek exceeds |0.01| in absolute value

Implied volatility opinion shifts by ≥0.50 volatility points from market level
exit_conditions: Underlying reverts to within 1.0 point of reference level
Skew returns to market consensus

rule_text: A trader should adjust volatility skew assumptions away from market-implied levels when their directional conviction differs, specifically by reducing out-of-the-money wing volatility when expecting lower tail-risk probability.
entry_conditions: Trader belief: probability of 2+ standard deviation move is <5% vs market-implied >10%
Historical realized volatility supports low tail-event frequency
No pending macro events or earnings
exit_conditions: Market-implied skew widens beyond trader's adjustment
Realized volatility of wings increases above historical average

rule_text: A market maker should adjust the volatility skew (shift levels or raise wings) in response to directional inventory imbalances to manage risk exposure across the strike spectrum.
entry_conditions: Net delta exposure exceeds 3 standard deviations of typical position size
Short OTM calls or puts represent >40% of total option position notional
exit_conditions: Net delta exposure returns within 1.5 standard deviations
Inventory rebalanced to within normal range

rule_text: A trader should compare the intrinsic exercise value of an American option against its theoretical cash-settled value adjusted for carrying costs; if intrinsic value exceeds the discounted theoretical value, early exercise is warranted and the option should be valued at intrinsic rather than time-adjusted theoretical value.
entry_conditions: Option is American (not European)
Intrinsic_value > (Theoretical_value - carrying_costs * 0.75)
Remaining_time_periods >= 1
exit_conditions: Exercise the option immediately when condition is met
Hold if margin shrinks below threshold

rule_text: A trader should exercise an American option early when its intrinsic value exceeds its theoretical option value, as the option has become underpriced relative to immediate exercise.
entry_conditions: For American Call: C(i,j) < 0.95 × (U(i,j) - E)
For American Put: P(i,j) < 0.95 × (E - U(i,j))
Verify option is in-the-money with at least 2+ periods remaining
exit_conditions: Execute early exercise immediately upon condition trigger
Hold if time value premium remains >5% of intrinsic value

son to a trader’s own volatility forecast. If the trader believes that 15% is a reasonable

rule_text: A trader should compare implied volatility against their own volatility forecast to determine whether IV is low, moderate, or high, then select strategies aligned with that relative assessment.
entry_conditions: Trader volatility forecast established
Absolute difference between IV and forecast >= 3.0%
IV materially low (IV < Forecast - 3.0%) OR materially high (IV > Forecast + 3.0%)
exit_conditions: IV converges to within 1.5% of forecast
Time decay reduces position value by 20%
Forecast changes materially (>2%)

rule_text: A disciplined trader should abstain from trading when lacking a conviction-based opinion on either implied volatility direction or market direction, waiting for conditions that support a directional or volatility thesis.
entry_conditions: Trader has explicit directional bias (bullish OR bearish with >70% conviction)
Trader has explicit volatility bias (elevated OR depressed IV vs. historical percentile with >70% conviction)
Both conditions must be satisfied simultaneously
exit_conditions: Position size to zero if either conviction drops below 70%
Exit if original thesis invalidated
